title: Fetal Weight Percentile Calculator
tagline: Calculate fetal weight percentiles by gestational age using Hadlock growth charts for accurate pregnancy monitoring.
intro: Enter the gestational age in weeks and the estimated fetal weight from your ultrasound to see where your baby falls on the growth chart.
formula: Formula: z = (ln(EFW) − ln(mean_EFW)) / 0.15 · Percentile = Φ(z) × 100 — based on Hadlock 1991 growth standards

error.invalid: Enter valid gestational age (14–42 weeks) and estimated fetal weight.

about.heading: About the Fetal Weight Percentile Calculator
about.body:
Fetal weight percentiles are one of the most important metrics in obstetric care. Rather than reporting an absolute weight in grams or ounces, a percentile places your baby's estimated fetal weight (EFW) in context among all fetuses measured at the same gestational age. If your baby is at the 50th percentile, half of all fetuses at that week of pregnancy weigh more and half weigh less. A percentile near the median signals that growth is proceeding as expected for the gestational age, while values at the extremes may prompt closer monitoring.

This calculator uses the widely cited Hadlock 1991 growth model. The mathematical foundation is a log-normal distribution: at each gestational age, the natural logarithm of fetal weight is approximately normally distributed with a mean defined by a second-degree polynomial (ln EFW ≈ 0.578 + 0.332 × GA − 0.00354 × GA²) and a standard deviation of about 15% of the mean. A z-score is computed by subtracting the population mean from the observed log weight and dividing by the standard deviation. The standard normal cumulative distribution function then converts that z-score into a percentile rank.

Gestational age accuracy is critical. A difference of even one or two weeks can shift the percentile substantially because fetal weight roughly doubles every few weeks during the second and early third trimesters. The most reliable gestational age estimate comes from a first-trimester crown-rump length (CRL) measurement performed before 14 weeks. Later ultrasound dating is less precise, particularly beyond 20 weeks, so clinicians typically lock the gestational age to the earliest reliable estimate.

Clinically, three zones guide interpretation. Fetuses below the 10th percentile are classified as small for gestational age (SGA). This group has a higher risk of adverse perinatal outcomes, including preterm birth, neonatal complications, and long-term developmental issues, so they receive more frequent growth scans and fetal Doppler assessments. Fetuses above the 90th percentile are large for gestational age (LGA). LGA is associated with maternal diabetes (both gestational and pre-existing), post-dates pregnancy, and constitutional large size. The majority of fetuses — those between the 10th and 90th percentiles — are classified as appropriate for gestational age (AGA) and generally continue routine monitoring.

Twin pregnancies tend to show divergent growth trajectories, particularly after 28 weeks, when placental sharing and uterine space constraints can limit individual fetal growth. This calculator applies a modest adjustment to the expected mean for twin pregnancies after 28 weeks to reflect these physiological differences, though dedicated twin growth charts (such as those from Gratacos or the INTERGROWTH-21st Twin Study) provide greater precision for high-risk multiple gestations.

Remember that a single out-of-range percentile does not constitute a diagnosis. Serial measurements plotted over time — typically every three to four weeks — provide far more useful information than a single data point. A fetus consistently tracking at the 8th percentile may simply have small but constitutionally appropriate parents, whereas a fetus that drops from the 45th to the 9th percentile across two measurements represents a significant deceleration in growth that warrants prompt investigation. Always interpret these results in collaboration with a qualified obstetrician or maternal-fetal medicine specialist who has access to the full clinical picture.

examples.heading: Fetal weight percentile examples
examples.intro: Click any example to load typical values into the calculator.

items.note: Appropriate for gestational age; median weight at 28 weeks.
Typical 34-week singleton at the population median.
Near-term fetus at average weight; normal AGA range.
Severely low — far below 10th percentile SGA threshold; prompt clinical evaluation required.
Above 90th percentile — classified as LGA; consider evaluation for gestational diabetes.

howto.heading: How to use the Fetal Weight Percentile Calculator
steps: Obtain the estimated fetal weight (EFW) and gestational age from your most recent ultrasound report.
Enter the gestational age in completed weeks (e.g., 28 for 28 weeks 0 days).
Type the EFW value and select the matching weight unit (grams, ounces, or pounds).
Select the pregnancy type — singleton or twins — and click Calculate Percentile.
Read the percentile result and its AGA/SGA/LGA classification, then discuss the findings with your healthcare provider.

items.q: What is considered a normal fetal weight percentile?
items.a: Fetuses between the 10th and 90th percentiles are classified as appropriate for gestational age (AGA) and are considered normal. Those below the 10th percentile are small for gestational age (SGA) and those above the 90th are large for gestational age (LGA), both of which may require additional monitoring.

items.q: How is estimated fetal weight (EFW) calculated from ultrasound?
items.a: Sonographers measure biometric parameters — typically biparietal diameter (BPD), head circumference (HC), abdominal circumference (AC), and femur length (FL) — and apply a formula such as the Hadlock model to estimate fetal weight. The EFW reported on your ultrasound is the input to this percentile calculator.

items.q: Why does gestational age accuracy matter so much?
items.a: Fetal weight nearly doubles every few weeks in mid-pregnancy, so a gestational age error of even one or two weeks can shift the percentile by 10–20 points. First-trimester crown-rump length dating is the most accurate method and should be used whenever available.

items.q: What does small for gestational age (SGA) mean?
items.a: SGA means the estimated fetal weight falls below the 10th percentile for gestational age. It may reflect constitutional small size (particularly if parents are small), placental insufficiency, or other causes. SGA fetuses typically receive additional growth scans and fetal Doppler studies to assess well-being.

items.q: Should I be worried if my baby is at the 5th percentile?
items.a: Not necessarily, but further evaluation is warranted. Your obstetrician will look at growth trends over serial scans, umbilical artery Doppler, and amniotic fluid volume to distinguish a constitutionally small but healthy fetus from one with true growth restriction. A single low measurement should be interpreted in clinical context.

items.q: Is this calculator a substitute for medical advice?
items.a: No. This tool provides educational estimates based on population growth standards. Individual results should always be reviewed by a qualified obstetrician or maternal-fetal medicine specialist who can integrate the full clinical picture, including maternal history, Doppler findings, and serial growth data.
